title: Viscous Settling of Bravais Unit-Cells
authors: Sebastian Bürger, Harshit Joshi, S Ganga Prasath, Rahul Chajwa, Rama Govindarajan
abstract: We study experimentally and theoretically the Stokesian settling of a well-known class of porous shapes: Bravais lattice unit-cells, whose porosity we vary controllably by changing their lattice spacing. In our experiments, conducted in a square cuboidal container with its long-axis aligned along gravity, we find that the settling speed U and the solid fraction φ of these lattice units obey a power-law relationship U $\propto$ φ^γ , with an exponent γ = 0.43 independent of their shape. To understand the observed scaling exponent, we analytically and numerically investigate the settling of the simple cubic structure under different approximations. We find that the walls of the container, though far from the sinking object, have a defining effect. Our Stokesian boundary integral simulations show that the Faxen's boundary correction captures the wall-effects accurately and enables us to discount the wall-effect from the experimental data, yielding a power-law exponent γ = 0.30 for settling in an unbounded domain. The power-law relating sinking speed and porosity is a step towards predictively understanding the sedimentation fluxes of complex objects in the clouds and the oceans. However, the applicability of this universal scaling to irregular and biologically richer aggregates found in nature remains an open direction.
summary: This paper investigates how the shape and porosity of complex, lattice-like structures affect their settling speed in a viscous fluid. By studying various Bravais lattice unit-cells, the authors discovered a power-law scaling between settling velocity and solid volume fraction. They specifically highlight how container walls significantly bias experimental measurements and demonstrate that applying Faxén corrections reveals a more universal scaling exponent. This work provides insights into the sedimentation of complex aggregates, which is crucial for understanding transport in oceans and the atmosphere.
main_problem: Understanding the relationship between settling velocity and porosity for complex, porous, non-spherical objects and determining if a universal scaling law exists.
main_result: The study finds a power-law relationship between settling speed and solid fraction, with an exponent of 0.43 in bounded containers and 0.30 in an unbounded domain after correcting for wall effects.
method: The researchers used 3D-printed Bravais lattice unit-cells in a viscous fluid, combined with high-speed imaging, Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV), Stokesian Dynamics (SD) simulations, and Boundary Integral Methods (BIM).
model_or_system: The physical system consists of various 3D-printed Bravais lattice unit-cells (such as SC, BCC, and FCC) made of spheres connected by slender rods, settling in a highly viscous silicone oil within a glass container.
key_observables: Terminal settling velocity (U), solid volume fraction (phi_s), and fluid velocity fields.
important_parameters: Low Reynolds number (Re approx 10^-4), lattice spacing (d), sphere radius (a), and the ratio of particle size to container size.
main_assumptions: The system is in the Stokesian regime; the SC lattice analysis assumes a far-field limit (a/d << 1) for certain models; and slender-body theory assumes rods do not interact hydrodynamically with each other.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the experimental setup and lattice types; Figure 2 shows PIV setup and streamlines; Figure 3 and 4 show normalized velocity vs. solid fraction in bounded and unbounded domains; Figure 5 illustrates the SD model; Figure 6 shows fluid velocity fields; Figure 7 compares experimental data to various models; Figure 9 shows the influence of rods on settling speed.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of porous object settling, describes the experimental setup and lattice geometries, presents experimental scaling results, details the numerical/theoretical models used to account for wall effects and rod interactions, and concludes with a comparison of the findings to existing theories like the Darcy-Brinkman model.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (a, b) Schematic of the experimental setup and the settling porous object: an Simple Cubic lattice made of spheres of diameter 2a = 6 mm which are connected by cylindrical rods of diameter 2b = 1 mm. The centre-to-centre distance between spheres is d. (c) Seven Bravais lattice structures we investigate in our sedimentation experiments: (i) Simple cubic (SC), (ii) body-centred cubic (BCC), (iii) tetrahedron, (iv) hexagonal close-packed (HCP), (v) face centred cubic (FCC), (vi) octahedron and (vii) square-based pyramid structures. Each of them contains one black-painted sphere to track the settling path, speed and orientation. For BCC and FCC, the lattice constant d refers to the unit...

FIG. 2: (a) Schematic of the setup of the PIV-experiment with laser-sheet bisecting the object, revealing the flows in the mid-plane between the four spheres in the front and their counterparts in the background. The extraction line indicates where the velocity profile in the PIV evaluation is measured. (b) Front-view of the PIV snapshot for SC structure with d = 10 mm, with suspended tracer beads in the background. Note that the PIV plane is different from the plane of the four visible cylinders, as Fig (a) indicates. (c) Streamlines from PIV images of the mid-plane showing circulation zones close to the boundary. The color depicts the magnitude of vertical component of the fluid velocity.

FIG. 3: Normalized terminal velocity obtained in the various experiments as a function of the structures solid volume fraction ϕs, where the porosity is (1 −ϕs). A universal scaling across the different structures is visible, with a power-law fit U ∝ϕγ s yielding an exponent γ = 0.433 ± 0.004. As described using eqn. (2), the velocity is normalized against the velocity of a sphere of the same mass, U1sph(mstruct/m1sph)2/3. Subscript ’b’ indicates the settling speeds as measured in the container, i.e. a bounded domain.

FIG. 4: Analogous to Fig. 3, but with wall effects removed for each data point in the bounded domain by using Fax´en’s boundary correction (subscript ’u’ indicating an unbounded domain). The results are now representative of settling in an unbounded domain (such as in the ocean or atmosphere), for which the power law exponent is γ = 0.296 ± 0.004. Note that with the exception of the fcc structure, the individual structure type’s exponents are in good agreement.

FIG. 5: Schematic of the setup used for the Stokesian Dynamics (SD) calculations. The larger blue spheres are of radius 3 mm while the string of small red spheres (of radius 0.5 mm) represent the slender rods. In our analysis, we consider two scenarios: retain the slender rods made of small spheres or omit the rods. The blue spheres are fixed at their relative locations in both cases.

FIG. 6: Velocity field lines on the PIV plane (yz plane) near falling SC structure in the bounded container, obtained using Boundary Integral Method (BIM). The finite container leads to backflow, resulting in the recirculating zones, also observed in experiments. The axes are normalised by the radius of the sphere a.

FIG. 7: Excess terminal velocity of the SC structure for different lattice spacing d, for experimental data and different analytical models. (a) The SD simulation for eight spheres in an unbounded domain show convergence against the Stokeslet prediction (9). The Boundary Integral Method (BIM) simulations in the bounded domain and the Fax´en corrected Stokesian Dynamics simulations for such eight spheres show good agreement between each other, validating the Fax´en correction. Furthermore, they align well with experimental data for small d/a ratios. (b) Whereas the Slender-body prediction (13) deviates substantially from experimental data, SD simulations of the full SC cluster with Fax´en...

FIG. 9: Influence of connecting rods on the normalized settling speed as a function of ϕs: Stokesian Dynamics simulation of eight spheres with and without rods vs experimental values. Note that the SD simulations suggest that the power-law behaviour continues to hold for the whole SC structure, the eight spheres without rods align well with the Stokeslet for small ϕs, with U8spheres →U1sph as ϕs →0, hence converging to 0.25 on the y-axis.

FIG. 8: Comparison between velocity profile for a SC structure at the extraction line obtained from PIV experiments (see Fig.2), and SD simulations (with rods, unbounded domain) as well as BIM simulations (without rods, bounded domain). The grey area indicates the interior of the SC structure. The velocity profile is normalized with the structure’s terminal velocity, which depends on d.
